ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general frog meat food product class; no specific entry for sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers frogs and other amphibians as a commodity group; no prepared dish-level code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bullfrog meat dish or sautéed frog preparation.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce is approximately 350 g, comprising bone-in bullfrog pieces in a generous chili-based sauce, served as a shared main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Frog, raw (NDB No. 17168) and analogous Chinese stir-fried frog dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy); supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for estimation of sauce contribution
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw frog legs (FDC ID 172960) was used as the protein and fat baseline. Cooked values adjusted upward for oil absorption (~5 g fat per 100 g cooked dish) and sodium contribution from soy sauce and doubanjiang (~450 mg sodium per 100 g). Carbohydrate contribution estimated from chili sauce components (~3 g per 100 g). Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based dishes — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database protein-dominant food category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce. The dish is protein-dominant (~14.5 g protein/100 g) with very low carbohydrate (~3 g/100 g). GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, low-carbohydrate stir-fried meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., stir-fried chicken and pork dishes with sauce, GI range 10–20). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (15 × 3.0 × 3.5) / 100 = 1.6, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and fermented chili bean paste (doubanjiang)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 450 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,575 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce versions is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce has a very low carbohydrate content (~3 g/100 g) and a negligible glycemic load per serving (~1.6), making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments; however, sodium intake from the sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content, partially offset by lean protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While bullfrog meat is a lean protein source with relatively low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g), the high sodium content of the chili sauce preparation poses a cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sautéed Bullfrog in Chili Sauce (辣椒炒牛蛙, là jiāo chǎo niú wā) is a popular Chinese dish featuring bone-in bullfrog pieces (Rana catesbeiana) wok-fried at high heat with a bold, spicy sauce made from fresh chili peppers, doubanjiang (fermented chili bean paste), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and Shaoxing rice wine. The dish is a staple of Sichuan-influenced Chinese cooking and is widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore, where it is prized for its tender, mildly sweet frog meat that absorbs the intensely savoury and spicy sauce. The texture of bullfrog meat is often compared to chicken — firm yet succulent — with a clean, mild flavour that pairs well with the umami-rich chili sauce. Nutritionally, bullfrog is a lean, high-protein meat (~14.5 g protein per 100 g) with low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g) and very low carbohydrate (~3 g/100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (estimated GI ~15). The primary nutritional concern is the high sodium content (~450 mg/100 g) contributed by soy sauce and doubanjiang, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is well-suited due to its negligible glycemic load. It is not halal-certified in most Singapore restaurants. The dish is typically served as a shared main course alongside steamed rice or noodles.
common_user_queries: Is sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce halal?
How many calories in bullfrog dish?
Is frog meat high in protein?
Can I eat bullfrog if I have high blood pressure?
Where to eat bullfrog in Singapore?
Is bullfrog meat similar to chicken?
meal_context: served with steamed white rice or noodles

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sautéed bullfrog in chili sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values were estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw frog legs as the protein/fat baseline, with adjustments for cooking oil absorption and sauce sodium contribution. GI was estimated by analogy with similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate stir-fried meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation approach rather than a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated: raw frog leg data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172960) was used as the protein and fat baseline; fat was adjusted upward by approximately 5 g/100 g for wok oil absorption; sodium was estimated at 450 mg/100 g based on typical soy sauce and doubanjiang contributions in Chinese stir-fry dishes. 2. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, and selenium are reported as -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the USDA SR Legacy frog legs entry and no reliable cooked-dish-level data was available from approved sources. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero as bullfrog is not a ruminant and the dish uses vegetable cooking oil, which does not contribute industrial trans fats. 4. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with high-protein, low-carbohydrate stir-fried meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for bullfrog dishes was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Meat and alternatives', which accurately reflects the primary ingredient (bullfrog meat). 6. Halal status is false as bullfrog is not universally accepted as halal in Singapore; consumers should verify with individual restaurants. 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315595 is used as closest parent (frog meat food product); no exact prepared-dish code exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Frogs and other amphibians) is used as closest parent commodity-level code.
